name: Grab Bars
shortDescription: Wall-mounted and suction grab bars for safer transfers in bathrooms and beyond.
intro: Grab bars are one of the highest-impact, lowest-cost upgrades for preventing falls. The right bar gives a senior a stable handhold exactly where balance is hardest — stepping into a tub, rising from the toilet, or steadying on a slick floor. We focus on weight-rated, properly anchored bars and explain where suction models are appropriate and where they are not.

name: Shower Chairs
shortDescription: Stable, height-adjustable seating for safer, less tiring bathing.
intro: A shower chair lets a senior bathe sitting down, which dramatically reduces fall risk and fatigue. We prioritize stability, weight capacity, drainage, and adjustable, non-slip legs so the chair stays put on a wet floor.

name: Transfer Benches
shortDescription: Bench seats that bridge the tub wall for safe sit-and-slide entry.
intro: Transfer benches solve the single most dangerous moment in bathing — stepping over a tub wall. The senior sits on the outside half, then slides across, never lifting a leg over a slick edge. We look for sturdy frames, smooth seats, and secure tub-wall positioning.

name: Raised Toilet Seats
shortDescription: Added seat height to make sitting and standing easier and safer.
intro: A raised toilet seat reduces how far a senior has to lower and lift their body, easing strain on hips and knees and lowering fall risk. We evaluate locking mechanisms, weight capacity, and optional support arms.

name: Bed Rails
shortDescription: Bedside support handles and rails for safer getting in and out of bed.
intro: Bed rails give a senior something solid to grip when getting in or out of bed and help prevent rolling out during sleep. We emphasize secure mounting, gap safety, and models suited to home (not hospital) beds.

name: Medical Alert Systems
shortDescription: Help-at-the-push-of-a-button devices for seniors living independently.
intro: A medical alert system summons help when a senior cannot reach a phone. We compare in-home vs. mobile units, fall detection, monitoring fees, and battery life, with a focus on reliability and ease of use under stress.

name: Pill Organizers
shortDescription: Daily and weekly organizers that make medication routines clear and safe.
intro: Medication errors are a common, preventable hazard for seniors. A good organizer makes it obvious whether a dose was taken. We look at compartment size, lid security, readability, and optional reminder features.

name: Non-Slip Bath Mats
shortDescription: High-grip mats that keep tub and shower floors safer underfoot.
intro: A quality non-slip mat is the cheapest fall-prevention tool in the bathroom. We evaluate suction strength, drainage, mildew resistance, and how well each mat grips real tub surfaces.

name: Large-Button Phones
shortDescription: Easy-to-see, easy-to-hear phones for seniors with vision or hearing loss.
intro: Large-button phones keep seniors connected and able to call for help. We prioritize button size and contrast, amplified volume, photo speed-dial, and hearing-aid compatibility.

name: Reachers & Dressing Aids
shortDescription: Tools that extend reach so seniors can dress and retrieve items safely.
intro: Reachers and dressing aids help seniors stay independent without bending, stretching, or climbing — common causes of falls. We assess grip strength, length, locking joints, and ease of use for arthritic hands.

name: Walkers & Rollators
shortDescription: Stable walking support — standard walkers, wheeled rollators, and seated rollators for getting around safely.
intro: A walker or rollator gives a senior steady support and confidence on their feet, indoors and out. We look for a sturdy frame, smooth wheels, easy folding, a comfortable seat where included, and reliable brakes.

name: Canes & Walking Sticks
shortDescription: Foldable, quad, wood, and bariatric canes that add a stable point of contact for steadier walking.
intro: A well-chosen cane improves balance and takes weight off a sore hip or knee. We prioritize a secure non-slip tip, comfortable grip, correct height adjustment, and — for extra stability — pivoting or quad bases that stand on their own.

name: Bedside Commodes
shortDescription: 3-in-1, drop-arm, padded, and bariatric commodes for safer overnight and limited-mobility toileting.
intro: A bedside commode shortens the risky nighttime trip to the bathroom and supports seniors with limited mobility. We assess stability, weight capacity, easy-clean buckets, padded and drop-arm options, and dual-use 3-in-1 designs.

name: Motion-Sensor Night Lights
shortDescription: Plug-in, rechargeable stick-on, and ceiling motion lights that switch on automatically for safer nighttime trips.
intro: Most nighttime falls happen in the dark on the way to the bathroom. Motion-activated night lights remove that risk by lighting the path automatically — no fumbling for a switch. We look for reliable sensors, warm glare-free light, and long battery life or easy plug-in power.

name: Compression Socks
shortDescription: Graduated compression socks that ease swelling, support circulation, and make long days on the feet more comfortable.
intro: Compression socks gently squeeze the legs to support circulation, reduce swelling, and ease tired, achy legs — helpful for seniors who sit or stand for long periods, travel, or manage mild edema. We compare compression levels, materials, fit, and comfort. Always check with a doctor before using firm (20–30 mmHg) compression, especially with diabetes or circulation conditions.

name: Low-Vision Magnifiers
shortDescription: Illuminated handheld, page, desk-lamp, and electronic magnifiers that make reading and close work possible again.
intro: For seniors with macular degeneration, glaucoma, or general low vision, the right magnifier restores the ability to read prescriptions, mail, and books. We compare illuminated handheld, rechargeable page, hands-free desk-lamp, wearable, and electronic video magnifiers on magnification, lighting, contrast modes, and ease of use.

name: Stair Safety
shortDescription: Non-slip carpet and rubber stair treads, anti-slip surfaces, and easy-install handrails that make every step safer.
intro: Stairs are one of the highest-risk areas in any home for older adults. The right combination of non-slip treads and a sturdy handrail dramatically reduces the chance of a fall. We compare soft carpet treads for indoor wooden stairs, all-weather rubber treads for porches and concrete, and easy-install wall-mount and freestanding handrails — with a focus on real grip, secure fit, and weight capacity. Physical therapists typically recommend a handrail on both sides of every staircase.

name: Door & Wander Alarms
shortDescription: Door chimes, exit alarms, motion sensors, and caregiver pagers that alert you the moment a loved one with dementia heads for the door.
intro: For families caring for someone with dementia or Alzheimer's, wandering is one of the most frightening risks. A simple door or motion alarm gives caregivers precious seconds to respond before a loved one leaves the home unnoticed. We compare magnetic door-sensor chimes, stand-alone door/window alarms, professional exit stoppers, motion sensors that detect an approach before the door is even touched, and caregiver call pagers — chosen for reliability, loudness, and ease of use. A layered setup (door sensor + motion sensor) gives the best coverage.

name: Overbed Tables
shortDescription: Height-adjustable, rolling, and cross-bed tables that make eating, reading, and recovering in bed easier and safer.
intro: An overbed table turns a bed into a comfortable, independent space for eating, reading, using a laptop, or keeping medications and water within reach during recovery. We compare hospital-trusted rolling tables, adjustable-length models that span queen and king beds, and tilt-tops for reading — focusing on stable bases, locking casters, smooth height adjustment, and weight capacity. The right table reduces the need to get up, lowering fall risk for seniors recovering at home.
